ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJX represents lentil soup generically; no specific code for Dal Daniya Shorba with coriander flavouring. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Dal Daniya Shorba or yellow lentil coriander soup.

main_ingredients: yellow lentils (moong dal or toor dal)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Dal Daniya Shorba is approximately 250 g (one bowl), as typically served in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Dal (lentil soup) — nutrient composition per 100 g
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition), Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, 2017 edition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked yellow lentil dal (moong/toor dal) entries. Dal Daniya Shorba is a thinner, more broth-like preparation than standard dal; values are adjusted downward proportionally to reflect the higher water content (~85–88% water) typical of a shorba (broth/soup) preparation. Coriander leaves contribute negligible macronutrients at typical garnish quantities. Adaptation is conservative and consistent with ICMR-NIN ingredient-level data.

gi_source.title: Lentil soup, GI testing data — lentils (Lens culinaris), boiled
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Dal Daniya Shorba. GI of 32 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled lentils (Lens culinaris, GI ~28–32, mean ~30) and cooked lentil soup preparations (GI ~30–44). A value of 32 is used as a conservative estimate for a yellow lentil broth-style soup. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 13.0) / 100 = 4.2, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 5.2 × 2.5 = 13.0 g.

hypertension.guidance: Dal Daniya Shorba contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g (525 mg per 250 g serving), which is moderate; individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and request reduced-salt preparation, as restaurant versions may contain additional salt or stock.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from lentils
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 32 and a glycemic load of ~4.2 per serving, Dal Daniya Shorba is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the soluble fibre in lentils slows glucose absorption and supports postprandial glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-based protein, and dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dal Daniya Shorba is low in saturated fat (0.2 g per 100 g) and provides plant-based protein and soluble dietary fibre from lentils, which are associated with improved LDL cholesterol levels; it is a heart-friendly choice when prepared without excess ghee or salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Daniya Shorba is a traditional Indian yellow lentil soup (shorba) flavoured prominently with fresh coriander leaves (dhania). 'Dal' refers to split or whole dried lentils, 'daniya' is the Hindi/Urdu word for coriander, and 'shorba' means broth or thin soup. The dish is prepared by simmering yellow lentils — typically moong dal (split mung beans) or toor dal (split pigeon peas) — with aromatics including onion, garlic, ginger, tomato, cumin, and turmeric until soft, then thinning the mixture to a broth-like consistency and finishing with a generous addition of fresh coriander leaves. The result is a light, fragrant, mildly spiced soup with an earthy, herbal character and a smooth, slightly thick texture. It is served hot, often as a starter or accompaniment to a main Indian meal. Nutritionally, Dal Daniya Shorba is a low-calorie, low-fat, plant-based soup providing moderate protein and dietary fibre from lentils, along with micronutrients including iron, folate, potassium, and vitamin C from the coriander. Its low glycemic index (~32) and low glycemic load make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The sodium content is moderate and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and halal. In Singapore, it appears on the menus of Indian restaurants such as Dumpukht Grills & Curry at Boat Quay, reflecting the Mughal-influenced North Indian culinary tradition of serving refined, aromatic broths as a course in formal dining.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Dal Daniya Shorba was found in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked yellow lentil dal data, adjusted for the higher water content of a shorba-style preparation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled lentils and lentil soup. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is transparent, conservative, and grounded in approved primary source data.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked yellow lentil (moong/toor dal) data, diluted proportionally to reflect the broth-like consistency of a shorba (~87.5% water). No exact match for Dal Daniya Shorba exists in any approved primary source. 2. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for boiled lentils (GI ~28–32) and lentil soup preparations (GI ~30–44); this is documented in gi_source. 3. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as selenium content for this preparation is not reported in ICMR-NIN or the adapted source data. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food containing no animal products; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant-based foods. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based soup with no fortification; vitamin D is naturally zero in this preparation. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this dish contains no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally zero in a vegan lentil soup. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used; confirmed zero for this preparation. 8. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record as lentils are classified under protein/meat alternatives in Singapore dietary classification frameworks. 9. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided as this dish is served in Singapore, satisfying the Singaporean cuisine multilingual requirement. 10. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no exact ontology entry exists for this specific dish.
